heroOverview: Pressure ulcers — also called pressure injuries, bedsores, or decubitus ulcers — develop when sustained mechanical load occludes capillary blood flow over a bony prominence, producing ischemic necrosis of skin and underlying tissue. They affect approximately 2.5 million patients annually in US acute-care settings and represent the second most common litigation target in hospital care. Up to 95% of pressure ulcers are considered preventable with proper repositioning, skin assessment, and pressure-redistribution surfaces. Staging from I (intact skin with non-blanchable erythema) through IV (full-thickness tissue loss exposing bone, tendon, or muscle) guides both treatment and prognosis. Unmanaged stage III-IV ulcers carry sepsis and mortality rates that rival major surgical complications.

keyStats.prevalence: Point prevalence in US hospitals approximately 2.5%; 2.5 million patients treated annually; prevalence in long-term care 4-13% (Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality 2022)
keyStats.demographics: Over 70% occur in adults aged 65 and older; nursing-home residents and ICU patients are the highest-risk groups
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age of affected patients: 70-75 years in hospital studies; median 78 years in long-term care
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 700,000 to 1 million new pressure ulcer cases per year in the US alone; global prevalence in hospital settings reported at 12-16% across systematic reviews

definition: Pressure ulcers (ICD-10: L89) are localized injuries to skin and underlying tissue, usually over a bony prominence, resulting from sustained pressure alone or in combination with shear force. The National Pressure Injury Advisory Panel (NPIAP) updated terminology from 'pressure ulcer' to 'pressure injury' in 2016 to include intact-skin injuries (stages I and deep tissue injury), but both terms are used interchangeably in clinical practice. The fundamental mechanism is ischemia: sustained pressure above capillary closure pressure (~32 mmHg) halts local blood flow; as little as 2 hours of unrelieved pressure on a vulnerable tissue triggers the cascade from reversible ischemia to irreversible necrosis. Shear forces — when two layers of tissue move in opposite directions — amplify damage by stretching the vessels feeding the skin from below. Moisture, friction, and malnutrition are cofactors. Common sites include the sacrum and coccyx (most frequent, ~33% of cases), heels (~30%), ischial tuberosities, trochanters, occiput, and elbows. Geriatric medicine, nursing, plastic surgery, and wound care specialist teams jointly manage pressure ulcers through prevention protocols, wound care, nutritional support, and surgical reconstruction.

typesClassification.description: Intact skin with localized non-blanchable redness over a bony prominence. The area may be painful, firm, soft, warmer, or cooler than adjacent tissue. Signals early ischemia; fully reversible with pressure relief.
Shallow open ulcer with a red-pink wound bed, without slough. May present as an intact or open/ruptured blister. Involves epidermis and dermis. Generally heals within 1-4 weeks with appropriate care.

Full-thickness tissue loss exposing subcutaneous fat. Slough may be present but does not obscure depth. May include undermining and tunneling. Depth varies by anatomical location; the bridge of the nose, ear, occiput, and malleolus lack subcutaneous tissue and stage 3 ulcers are shallow there.

typesClassification.type: Stage 4 — Full-thickness tissue loss exposing bone/tendon/muscle
typesClassification.description: Full-thickness tissue loss with exposed or directly palpable bone, tendon, or muscle. Osteomyelitis is common. Slough or eschar may be present on part of the wound. Highest morbidity and mortality; often requires surgical debridement and flap reconstruction.

Full-thickness ulcer covered by slough (tan, gray, green) or eschar — depth cannot be determined until the base is debrided. True extent of injury is unknown and staging waits until the wound bed is visible.

Persistent non-blanchable deep red, maroon, or purple discoloration over intact or non-intact skin reflecting damage to soft tissue below the dermis from pressure and shear. May rapidly evolve to stage III or IV through a normal-appearing wound surface.

primarySymptoms: Non-blanchable redness over a bony prominence that does not fade within 30 minutes of pressure relief — the earliest visible sign of stage 1 injury, occurring before skin breaks down.
Painful or tender area over the sacrum, heels, or other bony prominence in a patient with limited mobility, even when the skin appears intact.
Open wound or blister over a bony prominence, often first noticed during bathing or skin care, ranging from shallow stage 2 blisters to deep craters exposing muscle or bone.
Discoloration ranging from dark red to maroon or purple over intact skin in a pressure-prone area, indicating deep tissue pressure injury under a deceptively normal skin surface.
Wound exudate — serous, sanguineous, or purulent — from an open ulcer, often staining dressings or bed linens between nursing assessments.
Foul odor from an ulcer with significant bacterial burden or necrotic tissue, particularly in stage III-IV wounds with slough or eschar.
Fever, elevated white cell count, and localized warmth or erythema extending beyond the wound margins — signs of wound infection or early osteomyelitis.

Pain disproportionate to the visible wound in spinal cord injury patients with preserved sensation, or — paradoxically — no pain in patients with complete sensory loss who cannot generate protective withdrawal reflexes.
Sinus tract or undermining palpable by wound probe, indicating that the wound extends laterally beneath intact-appearing wound edges.
earlyWarningSigns: Braden Scale score of 12 or below on admission assessment — patients score poorly on sensory perception, moisture, activity, mobility, nutrition, and friction/shear, predicting high ulcer risk.
Reactive hyperemia (blushing red skin after brief pressure) that persists more than 30 minutes — blanching erythema preceding non-blanchable Stage 1.
Moisture-associated skin damage (MASD) or incontinence-associated dermatitis — macerated, erythematous, eroded perianal or perineal skin that substantially increases ulcer risk.
Localized skin warmth over a bony prominence detected by the back of the hand or sub-epidermal moisture detection devices — indicates early inflammatory response before visible changes.
emergencySigns: Fever above 38.5°C, rigors, and rising CRP or WBC in a patient with a stage III-IV ulcer — systemic sepsis from wound infection or bacteraemia requiring urgent blood cultures and intravenous antibiotics.
Visible bone, tendon, or joint capsule at the wound base — stage IV injury with high osteomyelitis risk requiring urgent surgical assessment.
Rapidly expanding wound with necrotic undermining and crepitus — necrotising fasciitis, a surgical emergency with mortality above 20% requiring immediate IV antibiotics and surgical debridement.
New urinary or fecal incontinence with a sacral pressure ulcer — proximity of the wound to urethral/anal openings increases contamination and sepsis risk requiring urgent wound care escalation.

causes.cause: Sustained pressure exceeding capillary closure pressure
causes.description: Capillary blood flow ceases when external pressure exceeds approximately 32 mmHg. In immobile patients, tissue over the sacrum can experience pressures above 300 mmHg in the supine position. Ischemia begins within minutes; irreversible cellular death occurs after 2-6 hours of unrelieved loading, depending on tissue vulnerability.

causes.cause: Shear forces from sliding or repositioning
causes.description: When the skeleton and deep tissues slide while skin remains stationary against a mattress or chair (e.g., head-of-bed elevation above 30°), shearing stretches and tears blood vessels in the dermis and subcutaneous fat. Shear injury occurs at lower pressures than compression alone and explains why heel ulcers develop even in patients turned regularly.

Repeated movement against rough bedding or clothing removes the stratum corneum, dramatically lowering the threshold for ulceration. Friction is a cofactor amplifying pressure and shear damage rather than a standalone cause.

Prolonged exposure to urine, feces, sweat, or wound exudate disrupts the acid mantle and barrier function of skin. Macerated, alkaline skin is three to five times more susceptible to pressure-induced breakdown than dry, intact skin.

Albumin below 3.5 g/dL reduces tissue turgor and repair capacity. Protein-energy malnutrition impairs collagen synthesis, immune response, and epidermal regeneration. Anemia reduces local oxygen delivery, lowering the ischemia threshold. Both are independently associated with pressure ulcer development in prospective nursing-home studies.

Tubes (nasogastric, urinary catheters, endotracheal), oxygen masks, limb splints, compression stockings, and pulse oximeter probes exert continuous localized pressure on skin or mucous membranes. Device-related injuries now account for 30-35% of hospital-acquired pressure injuries in ICU patients.

riskFactors.description: The inability to reposition independently is the dominant risk factor. Patients with spinal cord injury, stroke, hip fracture, or coma cannot perceive or relieve pressure and require full external repositioning every 2 hours.
Normal individuals sense pressure discomfort and shift position every few minutes during sleep. Sedation, anaesthesia, peripheral neuropathy, and spinal cord injury abolish this protective reflex.
Protein-energy malnutrition impairs collagen synthesis and wound repair. Patients below 75% of estimated caloric needs have a 50% higher rate of pressure ulcer development in prospective studies.
Moisture from incontinence triples the rate of skin breakdown at pressure sites. Fecal incontinence adds chemical and microbial injury to mechanical insult.

Aging skin has reduced collagen content, decreased dermis thickness, reduced resilience, and impaired capillary reactivity. Epidermis and dermis together thin by approximately 20% between age 20 and 80.

Peripheral neuropathy reduces pressure perception; microvascular disease impairs healing. Diabetic patients have a 3-fold higher rate of pressure ulcer-related complications versus non-diabetics in hospital cohorts.

riskFactors.factor: Acute illness or surgery with prolonged immobility
riskFactors.description: Hip fracture surgery lasting more than 3 hours on a hard operating table, prolonged ICU stay with sedation, or multi-day bed rest after stroke are recognized high-risk precipitants.

affectedDemographics: Nursing-home residents: 4-13% point prevalence in US LTC facilities; rates above 20% in residents with multiple comorbidities (AHRQ National Pressure Ulcer Database).
ICU patients: 8-40% incidence during hospital admission, driven by immobility, vasopressors reducing peripheral perfusion, and medical device pressure.
Adults with spinal cord injury: lifetime cumulative incidence of pressure ulcers exceeds 80%; sacral and ischial injuries predominate in wheelchair users.
Hip fracture patients: preoperative sacral ulcer incidence as high as 20% from time on emergency department trolley to theatre.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is primarily clinical, made by systematic skin inspection at admission and on a regular schedule throughout any episode of care. The NPIAP staging system (Stages I-IV, Unstageable, and Deep Tissue Injury) provides a standardized language for wound description. Full-thickness wounds require probing: a positive probe-to-bone test (touching bone with a cotton swab through the wound) has a sensitivity of 89% and specificity of 83% for underlying osteomyelitis. For suspected osteomyelitis, plain radiography is first-line (showing periosteal reaction and trabecular loss in advanced disease) but has low sensitivity in early infection. MRI is the gold-standard diagnostic tool for osteomyelitis — T1 and T2 sequences with gadolinium show marrow edema and bone destruction before plain X-ray changes. Bone biopsy with histology and culture remains the definitive test to guide antibiotic selection. Wound culture of surface swabs identifies colonizing bacteria but does not distinguish colonization from infection; tissue biopsy or deep wound aspiration provides more clinically actionable microbiology. Nutritional assessment (albumin, prealbumin, BMI, MNA-SF score) is performed at diagnosis and during treatment because malnutrition is both a risk factor and a barrier to healing. Braden Scale scoring quantifies ongoing risk and drives care-planning intensity.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Classifies wound severity by NPIAP stage and tracks healing by measuring length, width, depth, presence of tunneling, and wound bed characteristics
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A trained nurse or wound-care specialist inspects the wound at each dressing change. A ruler and wound probe are used. No anesthesia needed unless wound probing is very deep. Photography documents progress over time.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for osteomyelitis in full-thickness wounds over bony prominences
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A sterile blunt probe or cotton-tipped swab is gently introduced through the wound. Contact with a hard, gritty surface indicating bone is a positive test with ~89% sensitivity for underlying osteomyelitis. Takes under one minute; may be briefly uncomfortable.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging for suspected osteomyelitis — shows periosteal reaction, cortical erosion, and sequestrum in moderate or advanced infection
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard X-ray, takes under 5 minutes. Radiation dose is minimal. Sensitivity is low in early infection (2-4 weeks to show changes), so a negative X-ray does not exclude osteomyelitis.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI of bony prominence with gadolinium
diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard for diagnosing osteomyelitis and defining extent of soft tissue necrosis for surgical planning
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-40 minutes in the MRI scanner; contrast injected through a cannula. Gadolinium enhances areas of active infection. Results within 24 hours. Most accurate imaging for early osteomyelitis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies malnutrition as a treatable factor impairing wound healing
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw for albumin and prealbumin (results same day). The Mini Nutritional Assessment Short Form (MNA-SF) is a 6-question bedside screen. Results direct dietitian input and supplemental feeding.

diagnosticTests.test: Deep wound tissue biopsy with quantitative culture
diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes bacterial colonization from infection; identifies specific organisms for antibiotic selection in non-healing or clinically infected wounds
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small tissue sample is taken from the wound bed under sterile conditions, often after mechanical debridement of surface tissue. Local anesthesia may be used. Laboratory results take 48-72 hours.

treatmentOverview: Pressure ulcer management follows a structured wound-care hierarchy: first stop the damage (pressure redistribution), then manage the wound bed (cleaning, debridement, dressings), then support systemic healing (nutrition, infection control), and when conservative management fails, consider surgical reconstruction. EPUAP/NPIAP/PPPIA Clinical Practice Guidelines (2019) are the international standard. Pressure redistribution begins immediately with repositioning every 2 hours for bed-bound patients, and every hour for chair-bound patients, using a 30-degree lateral tilt position rather than 90-degree side-lying. Specialty foam or dynamic alternating-pressure mattresses reduce interface pressure by 40-70% compared with standard hospital mattresses and are mandated for high-risk patients. Wound bed preparation follows the TIME framework — Tissue debridement, Infection/inflammation control, Moisture balance, and wound Edge advancement. Debridement methods range from autolytic (moisture-retentive dressings) to enzymatic (collagenase ointment), mechanical (irrigation), sharp (surgical), and biosurgical (sterile maggots). Choice depends on wound depth, patient status, and wound bed characteristics. Dressings are selected by wound characteristics: hydrocolloids for shallow wounds, foam dressings for moderate exudate, alginates or hydrofibers for high exudate, antimicrobial silver dressings for critically colonized wounds, and negative pressure wound therapy (NPWT) for large, deep stage III-IV wounds. Osteomyelitis requires 4-6 weeks of targeted IV antibiotics guided by bone biopsy culture. Surgical reconstruction with myocutaneous flaps (gluteal, hamstring, tensor fascia lata) is offered when the wound is clean and the patient has adequate nutritional and circulatory reserve.

medicalTreatments.name: Pressure redistribution mattresses and heel offloading devices
medicalTreatments.description: Dynamic alternating-pressure or reactive low-air-loss mattresses reduce sacral interface pressure. Foam heel boots or heel elevation foam wedges protect the second most common ulcer site. Repositioning charts with 2-hourly turn schedules are the cornerstone of prevention and treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reactive and dynamic pressure-redistribution mattresses reduce pressure ulcer incidence by 40-60% versus standard foam mattresses in RCTs (Cochrane 2015); heel-protection devices reduce heel ulcer incidence by 55% in surgical patients

medicalTreatments.name: Wound debridement (autolytic, enzymatic, sharp)
medicalTreatments.description: Removes devitalized tissue, slough, and biofilm that prevent healing and harbor bacteria. Autolytic uses moisture-retentive dressings; enzymatic uses collagenase ointment daily; sharp debridement uses a scalpel or curette at bedside or in theater.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Time-to-heal reduced by ~30% with debridement versus non-debridement in systematic review of 11 RCTs (J Tissue Viability 2019)

medicalTreatments.name: Advanced wound dressings (foam, hydrocolloid, alginate, silver)
medicalTreatments.description: Selection based on wound depth and exudate level. Foam dressings manage moderate exudate; hydrocolloids promote autolytic debridement and protect stage II wounds; alginates handle high-exudate stage III-IV wounds; silver-containing antimicrobial dressings reduce biofilm in critically colonized wounds.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: No single dressing type is universally superior; clinical outcome is equivalent across modern dressings when matched appropriately to wound type (Cochrane Reviews 2017, 2022)

medicalTreatments.name: Negative pressure wound therapy (NPWT)
medicalTreatments.description: Continuous or intermittent sub-atmospheric pressure applied through a foam interface and occlusive film dressing. Removes excess exudate, reduces edema, stimulates granulation tissue, and manages surgical wound dehiscence.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: NPWT accelerates granulation tissue formation and reduces wound volume by 50-60% compared with standard dressings in stage III-IV ulcers in RCTs (Cochrane 2015)

medicalTreatments.name: High-protein oral nutritional supplementation
medicalTreatments.description: Protein intake of 1.25-1.5 g/kg/day is recommended by EPUAP/NPIAP for patients with stage II+ ulcers. High-protein oral supplements (e.g., arginine and micronutrient-enriched formulas) are used when dietary intake is inadequate.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: High-protein supplementation reduces pressure ulcer incidence by 25% and improves healing in stage II-IV wounds in meta-analysis of 14 RCTs (Clinical Nutrition 2020)

medicalTreatments.name: Systemic antibiotics for wound infection and osteomyelitis
medicalTreatments.description: Targeted to deep wound culture or bone biopsy results. Osteomyelitis typically requires 4-6 weeks of IV therapy (often followed by oral step-down). Surface wounds with cellulitis are treated with 7-14 days of systemic antibiotics; topical antiseptics manage colonization without systemic toxicity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Eradication of osteomyelitis with combined surgical debridement and 4-6 weeks of targeted antibiotics achieved in 70-80% of cases in published series

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical debridement and wound excision
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of all necrotic tissue, slough, infected bone, and bursa under general or spinal anaesthesia. Creates a clean wound base essential for subsequent reconstruction. Often performed as a staged procedure before flap closure.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Complete wound clearance achieved in 85-95% of selected patients; subsequent flap failure rate is higher when debridement is incomplete

surgicalOptions.name: Musculocutaneous flap reconstruction (gluteal, hamstring, TFL flaps)
surgicalOptions.description: Mobilization of well-vascularized local muscle and overlying skin to fill the wound cavity and cover the bony prominence with durable tissue. Gluteal flap for sacral wounds, hamstring flap for ischial wounds, tensor fascia lata flap for trochanteric wounds. Requires adequate nutritional and circulatory status.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Primary flap healing rates 70-85% in experienced centers; 30-day complication rate (dehiscence, partial flap failure, haematoma) 20-30%; long-term recurrence 30-50% at 5 years without sustained pressure relief

surgicalOptions.name: Skin grafting for stage II-III wounds
surgicalOptions.description: Split-thickness skin graft applied to granulating wound beds where flap reconstruction is not feasible. Provides wound closure but less durable coverage than full-thickness flap over bony prominences.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Graft take rates 70-90% on well-prepared granulating beds; higher failure with bacterial counts above 10^5 organisms per gram

alternativeTreatments.name: Photobiomodulation (low-level laser therapy)
alternativeTreatments.description: Pilot RCTs show 632-830 nm laser irradiation accelerates wound re-epithelialization in stage II ulcers by stimulating fibroblast activity. Evidence is preliminary; not yet standard of care per EPUAP/NPIAP guidelines 2019.

alternativeTreatments.name: Topical oxygen therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Continuous diffusion of topical oxygen through a sealed wound dressing has shown acceleration of healing in small trials. Addresses local hypoxia in wound bed without systemic oxygen effects.

whySeeSpecialist: A wound care nurse specialist, tissue viability nurse, or plastic surgeon should assess any pressure ulcer that fails to progress in healing over 2-4 weeks, any stage III-IV wound, or any wound with suspected osteomyelitis. Geriatricians coordinate multidisciplinary management including nutritional support and pressure redistribution for complex patients.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a tissue viability or wound care nurse (WOC nurse) for ongoing wound care management. For surgical reconstruction, choose a plastic or reconstructive surgeon with documented experience in pressure ulcer flap surgery at a center handling at least 20-30 such cases per year, as complication rates are strongly volume-dependent.
hospitalCriteria: Stage III-IV pressure ulcer with suspected or confirmed osteomyelitis requiring IV antibiotics
Systemic sepsis attributable to an infected pressure ulcer
Surgical debridement or flap reconstruction requiring general or spinal anaesthesia
Nutritional failure requiring nasogastric or parenteral feeding as an adjunct to wound care
Rapidly progressing wound with suspected necrotising fasciitis
keyFacilities: Hospital tissue viability or wound care teams
Plastic and reconstructive surgery services with pressure ulcer flap experience
Skilled nursing facilities with specialized wound care programs
Outpatient wound care clinics with negative pressure wound therapy capability
preventionStrategies: Reposition all bed-bound patients at minimum every 2 hours using a 30-degree lateral tilt; reposition chair-bound patients hourly — pressure redistribution is the single most effective preventive intervention.
Apply a pressure-redistribution foam or dynamic alternating-pressure mattress to all patients with Braden Scale scores of 12 or below at hospital or nursing home admission.
Conduct a full-body skin inspection at every bath or care episode, specifically checking sacrum, heels, occiput, elbows, trochanters, and any site under a medical device.
Manage incontinence proactively with containment devices or prompted toileting programs to eliminate prolonged moisture exposure.
Ensure adequate protein intake (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day) and caloric intake in all patients with limited mobility — involve a dietitian at admission if nutritional risk screening is positive.
Use heel elevation boots or foam wedges for all surgical patients with anticipated operative times above 3 hours and all ICU patients with peripheral perfusion impairment.
lifestyleModifications: Learn pressure-relief techniques: wheelchair users should perform push-up pressure relief for 30 seconds every 30 minutes; bed-bound patients require caregiver-assisted repositioning on a strict schedule.
Use a moisturizing skin barrier cream or spray twice daily to maintain skin integrity, especially over bony prominences and in the peri-anal area.
Maintain adequate nutrition and hydration: drink 1.5-2 L daily and eat protein-rich meals; report unintentional weight loss to the healthcare team promptly.
Inspect the skin daily if mobility is limited — or have a caregiver assist with inspection of posterior surfaces; use a mirror to check the sacrum.
Wear smooth-textured, well-fitting clothing and bedding free of wrinkles and hard seams that create localized pressure points.
Avoid hot water bottles and electric blankets directly against fragile or ischemic skin — thermal injury accelerates pressure ulcer formation.

recommended: High-protein foods (lean meat, eggs, dairy, legumes) providing 1.25-1.5 g protein per kg body weight daily to support wound repair
Vitamin C-rich foods (citrus, peppers, kiwi) — ascorbic acid is essential for collagen cross-linking and hydroxylation in healing tissue
Zinc-containing foods (shellfish, beef, pumpkin seeds) — zinc deficiency impairs epithelial cell proliferation and immune function in wound healing
Arginine-enriched oral nutritional supplements (Juven, ProCalAmine) — arginine supports nitric oxide synthesis and wound perfusion; associated with faster stage II-IV healing in RCTs
avoid: Excessive alcohol, which impairs neutrophil function and wound healing
Low-protein or calorie-restricted diets in patients who are already underweight or have stage III-IV wounds
Long periods without oral intake (nil-by-mouth orders) without nutritional supplementation — extended fasting accelerates skin fragility
Excessive fluid restriction, which concentrates urine and worsens incontinence-associated skin damage

exerciseGuidelines: Graded mobilization — sitting out of bed, standing, and walking — is the most effective way to relieve sustained pressure over the sacrum and should begin as soon as medically safe. For patients with spinal cord injury, passive range-of-motion exercises maintain circulation and prevent contractures. Wheelchair users benefit from seating assessment by an occupational therapist to optimize cushion selection and posture.
dailyManagement: Reposition on a written schedule posted at the bedside; document each turn with time and position to maintain accountability.
Inspect dressings for strike-through (exudate soaking to the outer surface) and change on schedule or sooner if saturated.
Monitor temperature and systemic inflammatory markers weekly — a rising CRP or temperature in a stage III-IV ulcer mandates wound assessment for deepening infection.
Record wound dimensions, photograph the wound, and update the wound care plan at least weekly to track healing trajectory.
Attend to bowel and bladder care at least every 2-4 hours to minimize incontinence exposure duration at wound sites.
prognosis: Stage I and II pressure ulcers heal in 1-4 weeks with consistent pressure relief and appropriate wound care. Stage III wounds typically heal over 3-6 months if pressure redistribution, nutritional support, and wound bed preparation are maintained. Stage IV ulcers may require 6-12 months of conservative management or surgical reconstruction, and long-term recurrence exceeds 50% without sustained postural management and pressure-redistribution equipment. Patients with osteomyelitis underlying a stage IV wound have a complication rate of 20-40% even with optimal treatment. The strongest predictors of poor outcome are continuing malnutrition, unmanaged incontinence, active infection, inadequate pressure relief, and advanced peripheral vascular disease. Pressure ulcers in the last weeks of life (Kennedy terminal ulcers) develop rapidly despite optimal care and represent end-organ failure of the skin rather than a preventable complication.
recoveryTimeline: Stage I: resolution within 24-48 hours of pressure relief. Stage II: epithelialization complete in 1-4 weeks. Stage III: full granulation and closure 3-6 months with optimal care. Stage IV: minimum 6-12 months; surgical flap closure may reduce time significantly but carries 20-30% complication rates. Osteomyelitis: 4-6 weeks of antibiotics; bone healing lags by months.
complications: Osteomyelitis: bone infection underlying stage IV ulcers occurs in 17-32% of cases; detected by probe-to-bone test and MRI; requires 4-6 weeks of IV antibiotics plus surgical debridement.

Systemic sepsis from bacteraemia: pressure ulcers are one of the most common sources of Gram-negative bacteraemia in nursing-home residents; mortality from pressure-ulcer-derived sepsis is 14-20% in older adults.
Necrotising fasciitis: rapidly spreading polymicrobial infection with gas in tissues; surgical emergency with mortality above 20% even with immediate debridement.
Contracture formation: prolonged wound care and immobility around a joint (hip, knee) leads to joint contracture, permanently reducing mobility.

Flap failure and wound recurrence: surgical reconstructions of sacral and ischial ulcers have 30-50% long-term recurrence in patients who cannot sustain pressure relief — the most common reason for reoperation.
Squamous cell carcinoma (Marjolin's ulcer): rare but recognized malignant transformation in chronic long-standing wounds; biopsy any ulcer that fails to respond to optimal treatment over 3 months.

supportResources.name: National Pressure Injury Advisory Panel (NPIAP)
supportResources.description: The authoritative US body for pressure injury prevention and staging guidelines, including patient and family education resources.

supportResources.name: European Pressure Ulcer Advisory Panel (EPUAP)
supportResources.description: International clinical practice guideline source (EPUAP/NPIAP/PPPIA 2019 prevention and treatment guidelines).

supportResources.name: Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality — Pressure Ulcer Prevention
supportResources.description: AHRQ toolkit for nurses and patients on risk screening, repositioning protocols, and skin care.

supportResources.name: Wound Ostomy Continence Nurses Society (WOCN)
supportResources.description: Professional organization for wound care nurses; provides a wound care nurse locator and patient resources.

confusedWithConditions.name: Urinary Tract Infection
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A sacral pressure ulcer contaminated with urine may trigger systemic signs (fever, elevated WBC) that are incorrectly attributed to a urinary tract infection. Distinguishing requires both urine culture and wound assessment; the two often coexist in incontinent older adults, and treatment of infection without improving wound care allows the ulcer to progress.

confusedWithConditions.name: Senile Osteoporosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Osteoporosis is a bone-density disorder that increases fracture risk from falls; it does not cause skin breakdown. Pressure ulcers develop from sustained surface pressure regardless of bone density. However, the same immobility that precipitates pressure ulcers also accelerates osteoporosis in frail older adults, and the two frequently coexist.

confusedWithConditions.name: Falls in the Elderly
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Falls can cause skin lacerations and hematomas that are sometimes confused with pressure ulcers. Pressure ulcers develop over bony prominences after sustained immobility; traumatic skin injuries from falls are typically found at impact sites, have different wound margins (irregular vs. defined), and have a clear precipitating event.

relatedConditions.name: Frailty Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Frailty reduces spontaneous repositioning, impairs wound healing, and increases the probability that a pressure ulcer will not resolve without intensive multidisciplinary support.

relatedConditions.name: Falls in the Elderly
relatedConditions.relevance: The same impaired mobility and neurological deficits that predispose to falls also eliminate protective pressure relief and increase ulcer risk.

relatedConditions.name: Geriatric Malnutrition
relatedConditions.relevance: Protein-energy malnutrition is independently associated with both pressure ulcer development and impaired healing; nutritional rehabilitation is an integral treatment component.

relatedConditions.name: Sarcopenia
relatedConditions.relevance: Muscle wasting reduces the tissue cushion over bony prominences and limits spontaneous repositioning, directly increasing pressure injury risk.

faqs.question: What is a pressure ulcer (bedsore) and how does it form?
faqs.answer: A pressure ulcer forms when sustained body weight cuts off blood supply to skin over a bony prominence, causing tissue death from oxygen starvation. As little as 2 hours of unrelieved pressure can trigger irreversible injury. The sacrum, heels, and hips are the most common sites in bedridden patients.

faqs.question: What are the 4 stages of pressure ulcers?
faqs.answer: Stage 1: non-blanchable redness on intact skin. Stage 2: shallow open wound or blister reaching the dermis. Stage 3: deep crater exposing subcutaneous fat. Stage 4: full-thickness loss exposing bone, tendon, or muscle. Unstageable and deep tissue pressure injury are additional NPIAP categories.

faqs.question: Are bedsores preventable?
faqs.answer: Up to 95% of pressure ulcers are considered preventable with repositioning every 2 hours, pressure-redistribution mattresses, skin inspections, incontinence management, and adequate nutrition. Hospital-acquired pressure ulcers are tracked as a patient safety quality indicator, and their occurrence can trigger investigation.

faqs.question: How long do pressure ulcers take to heal?
faqs.answer: Stage 1 injuries resolve within 24-48 hours of pressure relief. Stage 2 heals in 1-4 weeks. Stage 3 typically closes in 3-6 months with optimal wound care and nutrition. Stage 4 wounds may take 6-12 months or require surgical flap reconstruction; some never fully close without surgery.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for a pressure ulcer?
faqs.answer: First-line treatment combines consistent pressure relief (repositioning every 2 hours, specialty mattress), wound bed preparation (debridement of slough, moisture-balanced dressings), infection control, and high-protein nutritional supplementation. Stage III-IV wounds may require negative pressure wound therapy or surgical flap reconstruction.

faqs.question: Can a bedsore cause death?
faqs.answer: Yes. Stage III-IV pressure ulcers are associated with systemic sepsis, bacteraemia, and osteomyelitis. Mortality from pressure-ulcer-related sepsis in nursing-home residents is 14-20%. In patients near end of life, pressure ulcers reflect global tissue failure rather than a purely preventable complication.

faqs.question: What is osteomyelitis from a pressure ulcer?
faqs.answer: Osteomyelitis is a bone infection that develops when bacteria from a stage IV ulcer penetrate the underlying bone. It occurs in 17-32% of full-thickness ulcers. Diagnosed by probe-to-bone test and MRI. Treatment requires 4-6 weeks of IV antibiotics targeted to bone biopsy culture results, often combined with surgical debridement.

faqs.question: What is the Braden Scale?
faqs.answer: The Braden Scale is the most widely used risk-assessment tool for pressure ulcers. It scores 6 domains — sensory perception, moisture, activity, mobility, nutrition, and friction/shear — from 6 (highest risk) to 23 (lowest risk). Scores of 12 or below indicate high risk and trigger preventive protocols in hospitals and nursing homes.

faqs.question: How often should a bedridden patient be turned?
faqs.answer: Bed-bound patients should be repositioned at least every 2 hours using a 30-degree lateral tilt, not a 90-degree side-lying position. Chair-bound patients should shift weight every hour. All repositioning should be documented on a turning chart. Pressure-redistribution mattresses extend the safe interval but do not replace scheduled turning.

faqs.question: What dressings are used for pressure ulcers?
faqs.answer: Dressing choice depends on wound depth and exudate level. Stage 2 shallow wounds use hydrocolloid or foam dressings. Stage 3-4 high-exudate wounds use alginate or hydrofiber. Infected wounds use silver-containing antimicrobial dressings. Large deep wounds may use negative pressure wound therapy foam. There is no universally superior dressing type.

faqs.question: Does diet affect pressure ulcer healing?
faqs.answer: Yes. Protein intake of 1.25-1.5 g/kg/day is required for wound repair. Vitamins C and zinc are essential cofactors for collagen synthesis. High-protein oral supplements enriched with arginine reduce healing time in RCTs. Malnutrition with albumin below 3.5 g/dL is an independent predictor of non-healing ulcers.

faqs.question: What is a Kennedy terminal ulcer?
faqs.answer: A Kennedy terminal ulcer (KTU) is a pressure injury that develops rapidly in the final hours or days of life, reflecting end-organ skin failure rather than a preventable complication. It typically appears as a butterfly-shaped, dark-red or maroon wound over the sacrum and does not respond to standard pressure ulcer treatment.

faqs.question: When is surgery needed for a pressure ulcer?
faqs.answer: Surgery is indicated for stage IV wounds that fail to progress after 2-4 months of optimal conservative care, wounds with confirmed osteomyelitis requiring debridement, and deep chronic wounds where flap reconstruction is the most efficient path to durable closure. Patient nutritional status, smoking, and pressure relief capacity must be assessed before surgery.

faqs.question: Are pressure ulcers painful?
faqs.answer: Stage 1 and 2 wounds are often painful in patients with intact sensation. Paradoxically, patients with spinal cord injury or advanced dementia who cannot feel pain are at highest risk because they cannot generate the protective repositioning response that prevents ulcer formation. Wound debridement and dressing changes cause significant pain and should be pre-medicated.

faqs.question: Can pressure ulcers happen in hospital?
faqs.answer: Yes, and they are classified as hospital-acquired pressure injuries (HAPIs) when they develop or worsen during an admission. HAPI rates are reported publicly in the US as a quality indicator. Patients are most vulnerable in the ICU, during and after surgery, and within 48 hours of acute hospital admission.

symptomKeywords: sore on back from lying in bed
metaDescription: Pressure ulcers (bedsores): staged from skin redness to bone-deep wounds. Causes, NPIAP staging, wound care, prevention and surgical options reviewed.
